nameMeanings.name: Hale
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: topographic name for someone who lived in a (usually remote) nook or corner of land, from Old English and Middle English hale, dative of h(e)alh ‘nook, hollow’, or a habitational name from a place so named such as Hale in Cheshire, Hampshire, Lancashire, Lincolnshire, Holme Hale (Norfolk), Hale Street (Kent), and Haile (Cumberland). In northern England the word often has a specialized meaning, denoting a piece of flat alluvial land by the side of a river, typically one deposited in a bend. See Haugh . In southeastern England it often referred to a patch of dry land in a fen. In some cases the surname may be a habitational name from any of several places in England named with this fossilized inflected form, which would originally have been preceded by a preposition, e.g. in the hale or at the hale. This surname is also established in south Wales.
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Céile (see McHale ).
Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of   Halle  .

variants.name: Halling
meanings: English (Gloucestershire): habitational name from Hawling in Gloucestershire or possibly from Halling in Kent. Halling was named in Old English as ‘family or followers of a man called Heall’; Hawling may have the same etymology or it may have meant ‘people from Hallow’ (a place in Worcestershire named in Old English with halh + haga ‘enclosure’), or ‘people at the nook of land’, Old English halh (see Hale 1).
German and Dutch: variant of   Helling  .
Swedish: cognate of Hallin , formed with the suffix -ing ‘belonging to’.

variants.name: Halker
meanings: English and Scottish: perhaps from an agent noun based on Middle English halk ‘nook, corner, recess’ (Old English healc, healuc, a derivative of healh; see Hale ), denoting someone who lived in or by a nook. This surname is now rare in Britain.
English: variant of   Hawker  .

variants.name: Hallam
meanings: English (North Midlands): habitational name from Halam (Nottinghamshire) or from Kirk or West Hallam (Derbyshire), all named with the Old English dative plural halum ‘(at the) nooks or corners of land’ (from Old English halh ‘nook, recess’; see Hale 1). The name may also be derived from Hallam in Sheffield (Yorkshire), sometimes called Hallamshire, named from Old English hallum ‘(at the) rocks’, the dative plural of Old English hall ‘stone, rock’, Old Norse hallr.
English: possibly a variant of Allam with prosthetic H-.

variants.name: Hales
meanings: English (widespread, especially in the southeast): from the genitive singular or nominative plural form of Old English halh ‘nook, recess’ (see Hale ).
Irish: when not of English origin, this may be a variant of   Healy   or   McHale  .

variants.name: Haugh
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Connor, Eamon.
Irish (Clare): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEachach ‘descendant of Eochu’, a personal name which is probably a pet form of Eochaidh, Eachaidh (see Hoey ).

Scottish and English: from northern Middle English and Older Scots halh, haugh ‘a piece of flat alluvial land by the side of a river, typically one deposited in a bend’, also ‘river-meadow land’; either topographic for someone who lived near such a feature or habitational for someone who came from one of several places in Scotland and northern England called Haugh.

variants.name: Hallows
meanings: English: variant of Hale 1, a topographic name from Middle English hale, Old English halh ‘hollow, nook, sheltered spot’. The final -s may indicate an original plural, but in some cases it is likely to be excrescent, a post-medieval modification of the surname.

variants.name: Heal
meanings: English:

topographic or habitational name for someone who lived in or by a nook or hollow, or at a place called with this word (from Middle English hele, Old English heale, dative singular of healh ‘nook or hollow’). Hele in Somerset derives from this word, but the surname arises chiefly from one or more of the many minor localities named Heal(e) or Hele in Devon. In southwestern England and Kent this is an equivalent of Hale .
in the southeast Midlands of England, perhaps a variant of   Hell   1.

variants.name: Bagnall
meanings: English (Staffordshire): habitational name from Bagnall or Bagnall in Alrewas, both in Staffordshire, named with the Old English personal name Badeca, Baduca (from a short form of various compound names with the first element beadu ‘battle’) + Old English halh ‘nook, recess’ (see Hale ) or holt ‘wood’ (see Holt ). In the 16th century the name was taken to Ireland, where it became established in Leinster.

variants.name: Haile
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Ethiopian/Eritrean Lemlem, Senait, Bereket, Seifu, Abrehet, Amanuel, Berhane, Dawit, Dehab, Fekade, Fekadu, Hagos.

English: northern or occasional form of Hale , or a habitational name from a place called with Old English halh ‘nook, hollow’, such as Haile (Cumberland) or the frequent Hale (for example in Cheshire, Hampshire, Kent, and Lincolnshire).
English and Scottish: variant of   Hail  .

variants.name: Hail
meanings: English and Scottish: nickname from Middle English, Older Scots heil, hail ‘healthy’ or ‘health’ (Old Norse heill ‘healthy’). This surname may well have become confused with the much more frequent Hale or, especially in Gloucestershire, with Hailes . Alternatively, in some cases the name may derive from the Old English personal name Hægel, from hægel ‘hail(stones)’, which may have survived into Middle English.
South German: variant of   Heil  .

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament: ‘God is gracious’. See John .

en.lifeSummary: John Hale was born about 1585, in Watton at Stone, Hertfordshire, England, United Kingdom. He married Martha Kirby about 1606, in Watton at Stone, Hertfordshire, England. They were the parents of at least 4 sons and 1 daughter. He died in 1637, at the age of 53, and was buried in Watton at Stone, Hertfordshire, England, United Kingdom.

ja.lifeSummary: John Haleは約1585年にイギリスイギリスハートフォードシュアイアワットオン アト ストーンで生まれた。約1606年にイギリスハートフォードシュアイアワットオン アト ストーンでMartha Kirbyと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも4人の息子と1人の娘の親であった。彼は1637年に53歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスハートフォードシュアイアワットオン アト ストーンにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: John Hale(은)는 대략 1585년 영국 잉글랜드 허트퍼드실르 왓튼 앳 스톤에서 태어났다. 그는 대략 1606년 영국 허트퍼드실르 왓튼 앳 스톤에서 Martha Kirby(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 네 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1637년 53세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 허트퍼드실르 왓튼 앳 스톤에 묻혔다.

ru.Watton at Stone, Hertfordshire, England: ваттон ат стоун, хиртфордшир, Англия, Великобритания
ru.lifeSummary: John Hale родился около 1585 г. в местности ваттон ат стоун, хиртфордшир, Англия, Великобритания. Он женился на женщине по имени Martha Kirby около 1606 г. в местности ваттон ат стоун, хиртфордшир, Англия. У них было по крайней мере 4 сына и 1 дочь. Он умер в 1637 г. в возрасте 53 лет и был похоронен в местности ваттон ат стоун, хиртфордшир, Англия, Великобритания.
